Scenario.overview: Transportation Systems Complex A sophisticated yet basic-skilled threat group named CostaRicto executed a multi-stage attack on transportation systems, leveraging spear-phishing, credential theft, and control system disruption to cause operational chaos.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Financial gain and disruption of critical infrastructure

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: ["Social engineering", "Internet-facing assets", "Vulnerability", "Domains and subdomains", "Shared DB or directory", "Obsolescent products"]

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: Adversaries gathered intelligence on transportation control centers and employee details using public data sources like WHOIS records and social media.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: A domain resembling the target’s official website was registered to host phishing content and distribute malicious payloads.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Tailored phishing emails with malicious attachments (e.g., macro-enabled Word documents) were sent to employees, exploiting human error to deliver malware.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 448: Embed Virus into DLL
stage.description: Embedded macros in phishing attachments executed payloads to establish persistence on victim systems by exploiting unpatched software vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: Malicious scripts were scheduled to run periodically using legitimate system tasks, ensuring long-term access to compromised systems.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 8: Buffer Overflow in an API Call
stage.description: Outdated SCADA systems were exploited to escalate privileges, granting attackers administrative access to critical control systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code was injected into legitimate processes to hide activities from detection mechanisms like antivirus software.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: Credentials were extracted from compromised systems using tools like Mimikatz, enabling lateral movement across the network.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Internal networks were mapped to identify critical infrastructure, such as traffic control servers and backup systems.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 555: Remote Services with Stolen Credentials
stage.description: Stolen credentials were used to access additional systems via RDP, expanding the attacker’s footprint within the network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 168: Windows ::DATA Alternate Data Stream
stage.description: Operational data, including schedules and maintenance logs, was exfiltrated from backup servers using encrypted channels to avoid detection.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Stolen data was transmitted via HTTPS to evade basic network monitoring and maintain operational secrecy.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Control systems were disrupted by altering traffic signal timings and disabling communication systems, causing widespread chaos in transportation operations.

Scenario.summary: CostaRicto executed a complex attack on transportation systems, beginning with spear-phishing to gain initial access, escalating privileges through unpatched SCADA systems, and ultimately disrupting control systems to cause operational chaos.
